content.full_text:
RAVI MERTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
July 25,
1996
Larry Barnett
171 West Spain Street
Sonoma, California
95476
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-209
Dear Mr. Barnett:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) •1
OUESTION
May you participate in Sonoma City Council decisions
regarding the construction of a new hotel which will be located
approximately one mile from your bed and breakfast inn?
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the decisions regarding the
construction of the new hotel only if there will not be a material
financial effect on your bed and breakfast inn’s business and
property.
We do not have enough facts to make this determination
at this time.
We believe that the following analysis will allow
you to make the correct conclusion.
FACTS
You are a member of the Sonoma City Council.
You and your
wife are the owners of a six—room bed and breakfast inn located
one—half block from the town plaza.
Your operation provides a
breakfast, which is included in the room charge.
You do not have
a swimming pool, restaurant or room service.
1
Government Code Sections
8 1000-91015.
Commission regulations
appear at Title 2,
Sections 18000—18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.
428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916’ 322-S660


File No.
A—96—209
Page2
V
A developer has proposed building a 153 room full-service
hotel on the outskirts of town, with a swimming pool and
restaurant.
The proposed hotel would be approximately one mile
away from your establishment.
Given the relatively small number
of rooms currently available in Sonoma
(approximately 200) the new
hotel would represent an increase in rooms of approximately 75
percent.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participat
ing in, or using his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than
gifts.
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol
lars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
***
(Section 87103(a)—(d).)
As a member of the Sonoma City Council,
you may not
participate in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on any business
entity or real property in which you have an interest of $1,000 or
more.
(Section 87103 (a)
and (b).)
You own both the business and
property for your bed and breakfast inn.
Foreseeabi 1ity
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not


File No.
A—96—209
Page
3
required; however, an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downev Community
Development Corn.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983; fl I Thorner 1 FPPC
Ops.
198.)
According to your facts, there would be a significant impact
of a 75 percent increase in rooms in Sonoma if the new hotel is
built.
Since it is reasonably foreseeable that your business
would be affected by the decision to build the new hotel within
such close proximity to your business, it must be determined if
the effect will be material.
Materiality
The Commission has adopted several regulations to determine
whether a decision will have a material financial effect.
These
regulations contain different standards depending on
(1)
whether
the decision pending before the city council directly or
indirectly affects your economic interests and
(2) the type of
economic interest which would be affected by the decision.
Due to your inn’s proximity to the proposed hotel, your
business will be indirectly affected by the decision regarding the
construction of the hotel.
Regulation 18702.2 provides guidelines
for determining if the effect of a decision is material as to a
business entity in which an official has an economic interest.
You informed us that subdivision
(g)
of the regulation is the
appropriate threshold to apply.
Regulation 18702.2(g). provides as
follows:
(1)
The decision wil1”esu1t in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.
You stated that it is your belief that the development of the
new hotel will not affect the occupancy rate of your bed and
breakfast inn because of the difference in the nature of the two
facilities and the guests.
You therefore stated that your
business will not be materially affected according to any of the
factors in Regulation 18702.2(g).
You own the property for your bed and breakfast inn, which is
approximately one mile from the proposed hotel.
Therefore,
materiality must also be analyzed under Regulation 18702.3(b),
which states as follows:


File No.
A—96—209
Page 4
(b)
The reasonably foreseeable effect of a
decision is not considered material as to real property
in which an official has a direct, indirect or
beneficial interest (not including a leasehold
interest), if the real property in which the official
has an interest is located entirely beyond a 2,500 foot
radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries)
of
the property which is the subject of the decision;
unless:
(1)
There are specific circumstances regarding the
decision, its effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the official has an interest, which
make it reasonably foreseeable that the fair market
value or the rental value of the real property in which
the official has an interest will be affected by the
amounts set forth in subdivisions
(a) (3) (A)
or
(a)(3)(B); and
(2)
Either of the following apply:
(A)
The effect will not be substantially the same
as the effect upon at least 25 percent of all the
properties which are within a 2,500 foot radius of the
boundaries of the real property in which the official
has an interest; or
(B)
There are not at least 10 properties under
separate ownership within a ,500 foot radius of the
property in which the officIl has an interest.
*****
Under Regulation 18702.3(a) (3),
copy enclosed, the effect of
a decision is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which the
official has an interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period.
Therefore, for example, if the rental rate for the rooms at the’
bed and breakfast will be affected by $1,000 or more within a
twelve month period due to the new hotel,
and either of the
factors in Regulation 18702.3 (b) (2)
above apply, you may not
participate in the decisions.
In determining whether the decisions will have the effects
set forth in subdivision
(a) (3) (A)
or
(B)
above,
you must consider


File No.
A—96—209
Page 5
whether it is “reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
affect the development potential or income producing potential of
the property.”
(Regulation 18702.3(d).)
We cannot determine whether the decision to build the new
hotel will have a material financial effect on your business and
property.
Ultimately it is the public official’s responsibility
to ensure that the requirements of the materiality regulations are
met.
Therefore,
you must make a good faith determination,
by
applying the criteria in the above regulations, that the decision
regarding the new hotel will not have a material financial effect
on either your business or property.
If you determine that there
will not be a material financial effect on your business or
property,
you may participate in the decision regarding the new
hotel.
I trust this answers your question.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
W
1
6e}u
By:
Jil Steher
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JS:ak
Enclosure

QUESTION: May you participate in Sonoma City Council decisions
regarding the construction of a new hotel which will be located
approximately one mile from your bed and breakfast inn?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in the decisions regarding the
construction of the new hotel only if there will not be a material
financial effect on your bed and breakfast inn’s business and
property.
We do not have enough facts to make this determination
at this time.
We believe that the following analysis will allow
you to make the correct conclusion.
